title: Service Stored Procedures (SSPs)
text: Service Stored Procedures (SSPs) Uniface supports the execution of DBMS stored procedures by using the activate statement to address a Stored Procedure Component defined with the Signature Editor. This concept is referred to within Uniface as Service Stored Procedures . Service Stored Procedures provide a means of improving the performance of an application. For example, all complex calculations required for a query could be performed within a DBMS stored procedure, and only the result of the calculation needs to be sent to the client. You can execute DBMS stored procedures within Uniface by using stored procedure components. A stored procedure component is created using the Signature Editor. Service stored procedures are used for a wide variety of operations. Their main purpose is to build logic into the processing of SQL statements. For instance, if you want to select data from one of two tables on the server, depending upon a value from a third table, a stored procedure would be an ideal candidate. Pass-through queries allow you to use stored procedures. When your application is waiting for user input, it is not worth the added complication of service stored procedures to increase the speed, because neither the user nor the server benefits from the increased speed. Service stored procedures really become useful when more complex logic is required. For example, when satisfying a business rule that a particular book has to be in stock, and then checking to see if more books should be ordered on each purchase. Characteristics Stored procedure components have the following characteristics: The developer is responsible for the DBMS-specific implementation of the stored procedures. Because component signatures are portable over different environments, the ProcScript activation of a stored procedure is generic to all DBMSs. A stored procedure is called as a global operation, and not as a single instance. This means that the operations of stored procedure components are stateless, and must be defined as such in the Signature Editor. All operations defined as synchronous are executed synchronously. Uniface always ensures that a DBMS connection is established before a stored procedure component is executed through the use of the ProcScript activate statement. Stored procedure components can be executed locally (on the client) or remotely with the Uniface Server. Restrictions Stored procedure components have the following restrictions: The standard Uniface hitlist functionality is not supported. All data is returned in one step, and all ProcScript functions related to the hitlist functionality (such as $hits ) are not supported. Large object data types are not supported. For example, the Image and Raw data types and underlying segmented fields for entities. (Raw data parameters are supported in service stored procedures only on Sybase). Tables with overflow fields, and overflow tables, are not supported. There is no support for multibyte language character sets. Uniface special characters (such as wildcards, substitution characters, and so on) are not supported. Related Links Signature Editor The Signature Editor enables you to define, edit, and compile component signatures for both Uniface and non-Uniface components. Naming Specifications for Service Store Procedures For Service Stored Procedures, the signature of the Stored Procedure Component maps the names of Uniface components, operations, and parameters to the name of a package, procedure, and parameter names in the procedure.
